Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I was going to study, but then I'd just sit there feeling overwhelmed and not really knowing where to start. Sometimes I'd spend hours with my materials open but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I tried to put in around 22 hours a week, but a lot of that time wasn't really productive because I was dealing with so much anxiety and just feeling down.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, mostly depending on when assignments were due. I couldn't stick to a regular schedule because some weeks I just couldn't bring myself to engage at all. When deadlines got close, I'd panic and try to cram everything in at once. It wasn't healthy, but that's how I got through most of the semester.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline that made me log in. I'd realize something was due soon and force myself to check what I needed to do. Sometimes I logged in just to see if I'd missed anything important, but I wouldn't actually do much once I was there. It was more about managing my anxiety than actually wanting to learn.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my routine. I always had to force myself to go there, and it felt like a chore every single time. I know some people check it daily like email, but for me it was more like this thing I avoided until I absolutely couldn't anymore.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I'm a visual learner and I could pause them when I needed to. Sometimes I'd watch a video and things would click that didn't make sense in the readings. But even then, I only watched maybe 18 minutes worth on average because I'd get distracted or too anxious to continue.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time. I only posted four times the whole semester because putting my thoughts out there for everyone to see made my anxiety spike. I also skipped a lot of the optional readings because with 13 courses, I just couldn't keep up with everything and had to pick what seemed most essential.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course. Like if I knew it would help me on an exam or was required for an assignment, I'd do it. But if something was just 'enrichment' or optional, I couldn't justify spending energy on it when I was already struggling to keep my head above water.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required stuff I had to do no matter how bad I was feeling, but optional things were the first to go when I was overwhelmed. It's not that I didn't want to learn more, it's just that survival mode doesn't leave room for extras.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of PDFs and lecture slides and studied them on my phone or printed them out when I could afford to. I'd review notes on the bus or in bed at night when I couldn't sleep because of anxiety. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be lying awake going over concepts in my head, trying to understand things, but obviously that doesn't show up anywhere. I'd also study with a friend from one of my extracurriculars sometimes, and we'd quiz each other, but none of that peer interaction happened on the LMS.
Q11: All the time. I'd download something once and then never open it again on the LMS, but I'd have it saved on my phone and look at it multiple times. My actual engagement with the material was way higher than what my login frequency would suggest. I just preferred having things offline where I could access them without internet issues.
Q12: There were several weeks where everything felt impossible. My anxiety was through the roof, and I just felt this heavy sadness that made it hard to care about anything. I'd stare at the screen and nothing would sink in. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a little, but it was still a constant battle to show up.
Q13: Absolutely. With limited resources, I was also working and dealing with money stress on top of 13 courses. Some days I was just too exhausted to even think about logging in. The fatigue would build up and then I'd have these crashes where I couldn't engage with anything academic for days at a time.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be scrolling through course materials but not actually processing anything. My eyes were on the screen but my mind was somewhere else, worrying about bills or just feeling empty. I'd technically be 'active' in the system but getting nothing out of it.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt overwhelming or when I didn't understand what was expected. The anxiety made it worse because thinking about the task would make me feel sick, so I'd avoid it to feel better in the short term. I knew it was making things harder but I couldn't stop the cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. Without them, I probably would have done even less. I'd wait until the pressure was unbearable and then rush to complete things. It's not a good way to learn, but it's how I managed to complete 92% of my assignments despite everything.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. Sometimes getting a decent grade would motivate me briefly, but bad feedback would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. With my low resilience, it was hard to bounce back from criticism, even when it was constructive.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with so many courses running at once. I couldn't keep track of everything, and the LMS didn't make it easy to see what was most urgent. I'd miss things not because I didn't care but because I was drowning in information and couldn't process it all.
Q19: Having clearer priorities would have helped a lot. When everything seems equally important, nothing feels manageable. Also, the attendance requirements were hard for me because some days I physically couldn't make myself go to class. More flexibility would have made engagement easier during my low periods.
Q20: I think those students might be like me on my bad days, where you're trying to engage but nothing's sticking. You might be clicking around, watching videos, reading things, but you're too anxious or distracted to actually produce anything. The activity is there but the output isn't because something's blocking the process.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. My exam scores weren't great, but I passed. A lot of my learning happened offline or in my head, not on the LMS. Someone looking at my low login frequency might think I didn't try, but I was working with what I had, just not in ways the system could see.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through the motions. They also miss everything that happens offline, which for students like me with limited resources, is a lot.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just showing up or clicking buttons. For me, real engagement was rare this semester because my mental state made it hard to feel anything but overwhelmed. When I was engaged, it was usually brief moments of clarity between the anxiety.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting just to stay enrolled. It wouldn't show the panic attacks before logging in, or the sadness that made everything feel pointless. It wouldn't show that I actually wanted to learn but my brain was working against me. My data probably looks like someone who doesn't care, but I care a lot, I'm just struggling.
